answer:
Reviewer mostly right. But cut wrong axis.

Verdict: 1,200 words too short for war story. 3,500 unedited too long. Target 2,000–2,500, ruthlessly restructured.

Why author wrong: depth not same as length. Engineering-brand authority come from *specific* depth — real numbers, real failure, real fix. Not from prose volume. 3,500-word first draft always carry 30–40% throat-clearing: background readers already know, hedging, timeline filler. Cut that, depth survive.

Why reviewer wrong: 1,200 words force cutting war-story guts — the "here what broke at 3am, here graph, here why obvious fix failed" parts. Those parts ARE the authority. Best migration posts (Figma, Shopify, Discord type) run 2,000–3,500 and get shared *because* gory detail. 1,200-word migration post read like marketing. Engineers smell it. Brand goal dies.

What do:

1. Structure for skimmer, depth for reader. TL;DR box at top. Strong subheads. Graphs, code blocks, tables carry detail — cost fewer words than prose.
2. Cut: generic context ("what is Postgres"), justification of decision already made, anything reader Google in 5 seconds.
3. Keep: numbers (latency, cost, downtime), dead ends tried, ugly workaround, what would do differently. That last section — most-shared part, never cut.
4. Kill line: every paragraph must teach something senior engineer not already know. Fails test? Gone.

If after honest cut post lands at 1,400 — fine, reviewer was right about content. Lands at 2,800 dense — fine too. Word count target is proxy. Real target: zero skippable paragraphs.
